title-group.article-title: CT anatomy of the lungs, bronchi and trachea in the Mature Guinea pig (cavia porcellus)

aff.named-content: Anatomy Sector, Department of Basic science, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine
Department of Surgery and Radiology, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine
Department of Internal Medicine, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine
Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine

aff.institution: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceOmid Zehtabvar, Anatomy Sector, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Department of Basic Science, University of Tehran, Azadi Street, Tehran, Iran.Email: ozehtabvar@ut.ac.ir

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Since there is no complete study of topographic features of thorax structures in guinea pigs, this study strives to define the exact topographic features of thorax structures.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: The aim of this study is to provide comprehensive topographic information about the location of the trachea, bronchi, lungs and heart inside the thoracic cavity in guinea pig, as well as to study the features of these structures, their proximity to other organs and comparative anatomical studies with CT scan images in living samples.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Ten adult male and healthy guinea pigs were selected. CT scan transverse images were taken. The morphometric parameters of the lungs, bronchi, trachea, heart, thoracic cavity and abdominal cavity were measured.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In these studies, the position of organs such as the trachea, lungs and heart were monitored, and specified descriptions of CT scan images and anatomical observations were obtained. We realised that the heart in this animal was not inclined to the left, and due to the equal size of the lungs, the heart was almost on the midline. According to the measurements, 20.05% of the ventral cavity volume was thoracic cavity and 79.95% was abdominal cavity.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: According to studies on guinea pigs, the right and left have the volume, and the heart is located between them on the midline without a tendency to the left. It seems that one of the reasons for the reluctance of the heart to the left and its placement on the midline in the guinea pig is that the volume of the two lungs is equal. In the case of guinea pigs, both numerical parameters are smaller than rabbits’, but close to it. One of the most important factors in this study is that none of the animals were euthanised, and all of samples were alive after our study.

abstract.p: According to studies on guinea pigs, the right and left have the volume, and the heart is located between them on the midline without a tendency to the left. It seems that one of the reasons for the reluctance of the heart to the left and its placement on the midline in the guinea pig is that the volume of the two lungs is equal.

award-group.funding-source: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran

p.mixed-citation: Zehtabvar, O., Masoudifard, M., Rostami, A., Akbarein, H., Sereshke, A. H. A., Khanamooeiashi, M., & Borgheie, F. (2023). CT anatomy of the lungs, bronchi and trachea in the Mature Guinea pig (cavia porcellus). Veterinary Medicine and Science, 9, 1179–1193. 10.1002/vms3.113137017606PMC10188081

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: In many mammals, the anatomical structures of the internal organs of the thoracic cavity and the abdominal cavity have been studied, and significant differences within and between species have been recorded. There are significant differences in the anatomy of this region in rodents, which make up the majority of mammals. According to research on thoracic cavity anatomy and for the production of artificial hearts, CT scan has been identified as a diagnostic method in anatomy and pathology of the thoracic cavity and heart in carnivores (Henninger, 2003). Rivero et al. (2005) evaluated normal thoracic cavity diagnostic imaging in three adult dogs of the mixed breed. The aim of this study was to obtain information as a source of evaluation of the dog's thoracic cavity by the CT scan method. Cardoso et al. (2007) examined a dog's CT scan using Helical CT scanner and contrast medium. This approach is suitable for viewing lung parenchyma, vascular structure, bronchi and pleural cavities. Spiral CT scan is a noninvasive method for determining the types of dog lung diseases. Ohlerth and Scharf (2007) introduced the CT scan as one of the most valuable standard diagnostic imaging tools in neurological, cancer and orthopaedic diseases in small animals. They remarked that in addition to being used as an essential tool in the evaluation of tumours, the CT scan is also crucial in examining lesions inside, and furthermore, outside the skull, spine, skeletal structures, abdominal cavity and thoracic cavity.

Valente et al. (2007) used CT scan technique to examine the spine and the coelomic cavities of the sea turtle. The oesophagus, stomach, trachea, bronchi, lungs, liver, gallbladder, heart, spleen, kidneys and spine were assessable. Even the details of the respiratory and skeletal systems and the position of the organs of the coelomic cavity were well recognised. CT scans, along with other methods such as ultrasound, radiography, and biopsy, help to make surgery safer. Mackey et al. (2008) examined CT scans in animals such as reptiles, birds and small mammals. CT scan can evaluate internal structures without the superimposition of adjacent organs. CT scan images help diagnose the disease in time, prevent and treat the disease. The CT scan technique takes pictures of several views of the patient's target organ. This method may provide an earlier diagnosis of abnormal conditions and a more accurate assessment of the clinical situation.

Diagnostic imaging techniques are one of the best ways not only for veterinary clinics but also for anatomical studies. Various anatomists often use these techniques in their studies. The most prominent advantage of these techniques is that anatomical structures can be investigated in live animals. Diagnostic imaging techniques are very suitable for anatomical studies of the coelomic cavity organs of animals such as turtles that are difficult to access, and methods such as CT scans have been used for these studies (Zehtabvar et al., 2014, 2015, 2022a, 2022b). In addition, other methods such as ultrasonography have been used to study the anatomy of the heart structure in sturgeons (Zehtabvar et al., 2019, 2018).

Müllhaupt et al. (2017) evaluated the normal anatomy of the thoracic cavity of 10 rabbits using CT scan method. The aim of this study was to investigate normal general structures, especially the cranial part of the thoracic cavity.

In 2018, Kazemi‐Darabadi et al. examined the anatomy and CT scan of the lower respiratory tract in five southern white‐breasted hedgehogs (Erinaceus concolor). As a result, apart from a few exceptions like some blood vessels and heart details, most of the bone‐like structures and soft tissues of the thoracic cavity that were detected in the anatomical parts were also detected in CT scan images. They stated that CT scan images in this study could be used as a reference for other studies of white‐breasted hedgehogs as well as interpretation of thoracic cavity lesions and related structures (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). Ilgun et al. (2011) conducted a study on the macroscopic anatomy of the lower respiratory system in mole rats (Spalax leucodon). Zotti et al. (2009) examined cross‐sectional anatomy of the rabbit neck and trunk: comparison of computed tomography and cadaver anatomy.

Today, diagnostic imaging techniques are one of the best methods for anatomical studies. Among these methods, CT scan is a precise method for topographic and morphometric studies, especially about the structures of the respiratory system. One of the most important features of using these techniques in anatomy studies is that there is no need to euthanise the studied animal (Zehtabvar et al., 2022a, 2022b).

Nowadays, diagnostic imaging techniques are used in various animals for the purpose of anatomical studies, both in laboratory animals such as rabbits (Soroori et al., 2022), and in other animals such as ruminants (Masoudifard et al., 2022), horse (Goodarzi et al., 2021) and birds (Zehtabvar et al., 2022a).

Guinea pigs belong to Rodentia order. These rodents are from the Caviidae family and the Cavia genus. These animals are generally noninvasive and are suitable pets for children. They are also a type of laboratory animal that has been used for various studies around the world (O'Malley, 2005). Although the guinea pig nowadays is considered as a pet and is used as animal model, limited information about the exact characteristics of the anatomy of these animals is available. The four main research areas that guinea pigs are used in today are allergies and respiratory diseases, nutritional research, hearing and safety testing. Clinical examinations, as well as the diagnostic imaging techniques, are necessary to have complete anatomical information about the animal under investigation. These characteristics need to be considered in multiple studies. Furthermore, to provide the possibility of interpreting the harm to the heart and lungs and to better understand the relative position of these organs, the general radiographic images and CT scan are proper instruments to achieve the above goals. In radiography, the thoracic cavity is the most challenging area in guinea pigs in terms of diagnosis. First, because the thoracic cavity is significantly smaller than the abdominal cavity (O'Malley, 2005), the second reason is the ambiguity of the components inside the chest, resulting in poor contrast between the heart and the lungs in radiography. The cranial mediastinum is not usually seen, and even observation of thoracic trachea is sometimes impaired (Krautwald‐Junghanns et al., 2011). Differences in deposition characteristics between humans and experimental animals will influence extrapolation of toxicity results obtained from small animals to humans (Schreider & Hutchens, 1980).

As mentioned, one of the laboratory applications of guinea pigs is studies related to respiratory diseases, so knowing the anatomical structure and exact topography of different parts of the respiratory system of this animal is very important. Schreider and Hutchens (1980) described the morphology of the guinea pig respiratory tract; in this study, a morphologic description of the airways of the guinea pig was developed from measurements of casts of the lungs and nasal cavity and from measurements of frozen sections of the lungs. In the present study, features that have not been considered in their article have been addressed, for example, comparing right and left in each sample and comparing male and female guinea pigs; attention is also paid to the important features in the measurements in the CT scan images. Given that males are larger and heavier than females, it seems that there should be differences in the parameters measured (O'Malley, 2005). In Schreider and Hutchens's (1980) article, the casting method was used on euthanised samples. However, in the present study, the CT scan method was used in live samples, which seems to be more accurate.

The aim of this study is to provide comprehensive topographic information about the location of the trachea, bronchi, lungs and heart inside the thoracic cavity in guinea pig, as well as to study the features of these structures, their proximity to other organs and comparative anatomical studies with CT scan images in living samples. In addition, some morphometric items, such as the volume of different sections, will be measured. Another purpose of this study is to expand the use of diagnostic imaging techniques for anatomical studies. This could reduce the number of animals euthanised for studies related to anatomy.

sec.title: Individual
sec.p: In this study, 10 adult male clinically healthy English guinea pigs (Cavia porcellus) were used for CT scan study; due consideration was given to minimise the number of animals used. The average weight of the animals was 1100.50 g ± 24.40 (mean± standard error).

All the guinea pigs were kept at the Razi Research Institute (laboratory animals department) under the same standard living conditions; animals are housed one per cage and have free access to food and water (Röder & Timmermans, 2002). For our study, animals were transferred to the Department of Anatomy at the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran. Five adult male cadavers were used for gross anatomical study; these guinea pigs were euthanised because of some nonrespiratory diseases in the veterinary hospital and were transferred to the Department of Anatomy at the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine for our study. No guinea pigs were euthanised for this study. As explained below, all guinea pigs were anaesthetised and regained consciousness after the study. In this study, no guinea pigs were killed for anatomical studies such as cross‐sections similar to CT scans. This is because due to the accuracy of CT scan images and anatomical studies in the past and their comparison with CT scan images in different animals, different anatomical features can be detected and measured with high accuracy using CT scans.

sec.title: CT scan study
sec.p: After transferring the individuals to the small animal veterinary hospital of Faculty of Veterinary Medicine (University of Tehran), the guinea pigs were anaesthetised using ketamine (10% alfasan, 30 mg/kg, intramuscular) and diazepam (10 mg/2 mL, caspian, 3 mg/kg, intramuscular) (Carpenter & Marion, 2018). In this study, thoracic CT scan was performed using a helical scanner (Somatom Spirit Series II CT scanner, Siemens, Germany).

All animals were placed on the CT scan machine table with abdominal recumbency. All the scans were prepared from the head to the beginning of the tail. At this stage, images were taken transversely perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the body (the spine). To study each section, the appropriate Window width (WW) and Window level (WL) were selected. Technical CT factors were as follows: rotation time, 1s; slice thickness, 1 mm; repair interval, 0.5–1 mm; pitch, 1; X‐ray tube potential, 120 kV; and X‐ray tube current, 130 mA. The bone and lung window were used to examine the images. Furthermore, 3D images were also reconstructed using the Osseous‐Shaded‐vp, pulmonary vascularisation and onco‐pulmonary‐vp pattern pattern. Syngo MMWP VE40A software was used for 3D reconstruction of digital images.

sec.title: Morphometric study
sec.p: Morphometric mensuration from digital CT images was performed with Syngo MMWP VE40A software. Statistical analyses were done by SPSS package Version 24.0. The data were described by means ± SE. The paired t test was performed to analyse the differences between the paired indices. A p value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Measurements were done once and by the same person. Anatomic structures were identified based on references (Popesko et al., 1992). The measured parameters and their descriptions are shown in the Table 1.

caption.p: Description of the measured morphometric parameters.

tr.td: The diameter of the bronchus was measured immediately after branching from the trachea
In the 3D reconstructed CT scan images with pulmonary pattern, the maximal length was measured from apex to the most caudal point of the lung diaphragmatic border
In the 3D reconstructed CT scan images with pulmonary pattern, the maximal width was measured
In the 3D reconstructed CT scan images with pulmonary pattern, the maximal height was measured
This parameter was measured using MMWP VE40A software, Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −700 to −600 (HU for lung)
It was measured from the tracheal bifurcation to the entry of bronchus into the lung, In the 3D reconstructed CT scan images with pulmonary pattern,
It was measured from the entry of trachea into the thorax to the tracheal bifurcation, In the 3D reconstructed CT scan images with pulmonary pattern
This parameter was measured using MMWP VE40A software, Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −120 to 60 (HU for oil and soft tissues)
This parameter was measured using MMWP VE40A software, Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −700 to 60 (HU for oil, lung and soft tissues)
This parameter was measured using MMWP VE40A software, Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −1000 to 60 (HU for air, oil and soft tissues)
In the midsagittal CT scan image, the cardiac angle was determined from heart base to the apex, along the lumen of the left ventricle
In the sagittal plane long axis of the heart was measured from the ventral border of the tracheal bifurcation to the cardiac apex
In the midsagittal plane of the caudal vena cava, this parameter was measured along the atrioventricular valves
Relative cardiac height (ratio maximal cardiac height/thoracic height)
This parameter was calculated on sagittal images as the ratio of the maximal cardiac height measured along the ribs to the thoracic height at the same level
Relative cardiac width (ratio maximal cardiac width/thoracic width)
This parameter was calculated as a ratio of the maximal cardiac width to the thoracic width at the same level, in the coronal CT scan image

The long and short axis dimensions were added, transposed onto the vertebral column and recorded as the number of vertebrae starting at the cranial edge of the 4th thoracic vertebra. The given distance along the vertebral column to the caudal point was estimated as the number of vertebrae resulting in a vertebral heart score
The angle between the tangent to the dorsal edge of the thoracic trachea and the tangent to the ventral edge of the thoracic part of the vertebral column
To measure this parameter, tracheal height was measured at the entrance to the thorax near the first thoracic vertebrae
To measure this parameter, tracheal width was measured at the entrance to the thorax near the first thoracic vertebrae
Ratio tracheal height/thoracic height at thoracic inlet
The ratio of the tracheal height/thoracic height at thoracic inlet
Cross‐sectional area (CSA) of tracheal lumen at bifurcation
This parameter was measured just cranial to the tracheal bifurcation. MMWP VE40A software was used for measurement; Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −1000 (HU for air)
Luminal CSA of right and left principal bronchus
Caudal to the tracheal bifurcation, the CSA of the lumen of the right and left principal bronchus was evaluated; Hounsfield unit (HU) was adjusted −1000 (HU for air)
In the coronal CT scan image, the interbronchial angle between the mid‐axis of both principal bronchi was assessed
Density of right and left caudal lung

Lung density (HU) was determined on CT scan images in a defined area of 50 mm2 dorsolateral to the right and left caudal lobar bronchi avoiding the inclusion of vessels. This parameter was measured using MMWP VE40A software.

sec.title: Gross anatomical study
sec.p: For this part of the study, five adult male dead guinea pigs (had died in last 2 years and were brought to our department, 1095.44 g ± 21.44) were used. Of course, we must mention that these samples were not used for measurements and were only studied to check the structure of the lobes of the lungs. An incision was made on the neck, which continued until the end of the last rib. The sternum was removed entirely to facilitate the entrance to the thorax area and to facilitate the study of the anatomical position of the heart and lungs. In examining the structures in the thorax, we concentrate on their various parts and on their beginning and end limits.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: In this study, the location of the trachea, lungs and heart were studied using the usual methods of anatomical examination, two‐dimensional and three‐dimensional CT scan, and the connections between them were determined. There was also no conflict between CT scan findings and gross anatomy observations. It should be noted that in this section, the order of reference to different structures is based on the order of their observation in the Gross Anatomical Study stages.

sec.title: CT scan study
sec.p: The thorax in the adult English guinea pig had 14 pairs of ribs, the last 5 pairs of which were floating. The sternum of this animal consisted of 5 sternebrae. The first 6 pairs of ribs were sternal and the next 8 pairs were asternal. The thoracic cavity was small in comparison with the size of the animal (Figure 1).

caption.p: 3D reconstructed CT scan images of the thorax with osseous shaded‐vp pattern, Mature English guinea pig, with forelimb skeleton (a), without forelimb skeleton (b).

sec.p: The trachea on the midline was started from the larynx. Along the neck, it was almost close to the midline and deviated slightly to the right. The trachea entered the thoracic cavity on the right side of the midline. After entering the thoracic cavity, after the level of the second rib, it deviated more to the right, and on the right, at the level of the space between the fifth and sixth ribs, close to the cranial border of the sixth rib, it was divided into two branches (right and left primary bronchus) (Figures 2 and 3) (Table 2).

caption.p: Transverse CT scan images of the thorax (Bone window), Mature English guinea pig, in 3D image the section sites have been marked. 1. Scapula, 2. 3rd thoracic vertebra, 3. 3rd rib, 4. left lung, 5. trachea, 6. right lung, 7. sternum, 8. 4th thoracic vertebra, 9. 4th rib, 10. heart, 11. tracheal bifurcation, 12. 5th thoracic vertebra, 13. 5th rib, 14. secondary bronchus (supply the right cranial lobe), 15. left primary bronchus, 16. 6th rib, 17. 7th rib, 18. secondary bronchus (supply the left middle lobe), 19. 8th rib, 20. caudal venacava, 21. 11th rib, 22. stomach, 23. 12th rib. 24. right primary bronchus.

Transverse CT scan images of the thorax (Lung window), Mature English guinea pig, in 3D image the section sites have been marked. 1. Scapula, 2. 3rd thoracic vertebra, 3. 3rd rib, 4. left lung, 5. trachea, 6. right lung, 7. sternum, 8. 4th thoracic vertebra, 9. 4th rib, 10. heart, 11. tracheal bifurcation, 12. 5th thoracic vertebra, 13. 5th rib, 14. right primary bronchus or right principal bronchus (lung entrance), 15. left primary bronchus or left principal bronchus, 16. 6th rib, 17. 7th rib, 18. secondary bronchus (supply the left middle lobe), 19. 8th rib, 20. caudal venacava, 21. 11th rib, 22. stomach, 23. 12th rib.
The positioning of the organs in the thoracic cavity, Mature English guinea pig.

tr.td: From 5th or 6th rib to 9th or 10th rib
From 5th or 6th rib to 9th or 10th rib
From caudal border of the 3rd rib to 12th rib

sec.p: The trachea continued from the beginning of the entrance to the thoracic cavity to the bifurcation site caudodorsally and inclined to the right. The right primary bronchus entered the lungs at the level between the sixth and seventh ribs. The left primary bronchus enters the lungs at the level between the seventh seventh and eighth ribs, near the seventh rib (Figures 2, 3, 4 and 5) (Table 2).

caption.p: Frontal CT scan images of the thorax (Lung window), Mature English guinea pig, in 3D image the section sites have been marked. 1. Stomach, 2. right lung, 3. left lung, 4. pulmonary vessel, 5. lobar bronchus (secondary bronchus), 6. tracheal bifurcation, 7. principal bronchus, 8. trachea, 9. caudal venacava, 10. heart.

3D reconstructed CT scan images of the lungs, trachea and left, right bronchus with onco‐pulmonary‐vp pattern, Dorsal view, Mature English guinea pig 1. Trachea, 2. left lung, 3. right lung, 4. left primary bronchus, 5. right primary bronchus.

sec.p: The right first primary bronchus branches to three secondary bronchi, which supply the cranial, middle and accessory lobes, and then terminates with the right caudal lobe. First right secondary bronchus supplies the cranial lobe, this lobar bronchus branches from the right primary bronchus at the level of the sixth right rib. This bronchus was directed dorsolaterally (Figure 6). Second secondary bronchus supplies the middle lobe, this lobar bronchus branches from the right primary bronchus at the level of the seventh right rib. This bronchus was directed ventrolaterally (Figure 6). Third secondary bronchus supplies the accessory lobe, this lobar bronchus branches from the right primary bronchus at the level of the right 8–9 intercostal space. This bronchus was directed ventrolaterally (Figure 6).

caption.p: Transverse CT scan images of the thorax (Lung window), Mature English guinea pig. In this figure, more attention is paid to the branches of the secondary bronchi. The image has been enlarged to better see the details: (a) at the level of the 6th rib, (b) at the level of the 7th rib, (c) at the level of the 8–9 intercostal space, (d) at the level of the 9th rib. 1. Left primary bronchus, 2. right primary bronchus, 3. first right secondary bronchus, 4. second right secondary bronchus, 5. first left secondary bronchus, 6. third right secondary bronchus, 7. second left secondary bronchus.

sec.p: The left first primary bronchus branches to two secondary bronchi which supply the cranial and middle lobes, and then terminates with the left caudal lobe. First left secondary bronchus supplies the cranial lobe, this lobar bronchus branches from the left primary bronchus at the level of the seventh left rib. This bronchus was directed ventrolaterally (Figure 6). Second left secondary bronchus supplies the middle lobe, this lobar bronchus branches from the left primary bronchus at the level of the ninth left rib. This bronchus was directed ventrolaterally (Figure 6).

Both lungs began from the level of the space between the third and fourth ribs (the left lung from the third rib and the right lung near the caudal border in the third rib). The right lung continued to the vicinity of the twelfth rib and the left lung to the region of the eleventh rib (Figure 7) (Table 2).

caption.p: 3D reconstructed CT scan images of the thorax with pulmonary vascularisation pattern (a, b) and pulmonary pattern (c, d), Mature English guinea pig, Right and left view of the body without section (a, b). Medial view of the sagittal section of the body (c, d). 1. Right lung, 2. left lung, 3. stomach, 4. trachea.

sec.p: The cranial part of the heart was almost on the midline of the body, and the caudal part was almost on the midline (Figure 2). In these samples, the heart was located at the level of the fourth and ninth ribs (Table 2).

sec.title: Gross anatomical study
sec.p: The right lung had four lobes, including the cranial, middle, caudal and accessory. The left lung also has three lobes, including the cranial, middle and caudal (Figures 8 and 9). The heart in these animals was conical, and the placement of the heart was from top to bottom and inclined caudally (Figures 2, 3, 4, 10).

caption.p: Ventral view of the lungs, Mature English guinea pig. 1. Trachea, 2. left cranial lobe, 3. left middle lobe, 4. left caudal lobe, 5. right cranial lobe, 6. right middle lobe, 7. right caudal lobe, 8. right accessory lobe.
Dorsal view of the lungs, Mature English guinea pig. 1. Trachea, 2. right cranial lobe, 3. right middle lobe, 4. right caudal lobe, 5. left cranial lobe, 6. left caudal lobe.

Sagittal CT scan images of the thorax (Lung window), Mature English guinea pig, in 3D image the section sites have been marked. 1. Trachea, 2. right lung, 3. heart, 4. pulmonary vessel, 5. tracheal bifurcation, 6. caudal venacava, 7. right primary bronchus, 8. lobar bronchus, 9. stomach, 10. left lung.

sec.title: Morphometric study
sec.p: The results of the measurements are shown in Table 3. The results of the statistical analysis of the measurements and statistical studies are shown in Table 4. The difference in size between right and left lung length was statistically significant, and the right lung had larger length (Tables 3 and 4) (p < 0.001). The difference in size between right and left lung width, height and volume was not statistically significant, and the right and left lung were equal in width, height and volume (Tables 3 and 4) (p > 0.001). The difference in size between right and left lung length was statistically significant, and the right lung had larger length (Tables 3 and 4) (p < 0.001). The difference in size between density of right and left caudal lung lobe was statistically significant, and the left caudal lung lobe had more negative density (Tables 3 and 4) (p < 0.001).

caption.p: Morphometric studies results, Mature English guinea pig (SE: standard error).

tr.td: Length of the left primary bronchus
Length of the right primary bronchus
Diameter of the left primary bronchus
Diameter of the right primary bronchus
Luminal CSA of right principal bronchus
Luminal CSA of left principal bronchus
Ratio tracheal height/thoracic height at thoracic inlet
CSA of tracheal lumen at bifurcation

fn.p: SE, standard error; CSA, cross‐sectional area; HU, Hounsfield unit.

caption.p: Morphometric studies results, Mature English guinea pig (SE: standard error).

tr.td: Length of the right lung–length of the left lung
Width of the left lung–width of the right lung
Height of the left lung—height of the right lung
Volume of the left lung–volume of the right lung
Density of right caudal lung lobe—density of left caudal lung lobe
Length of the left primary bronchus–length of the right primary bronchus
Diameter of the left primary bronchus–diameter of the right primary bronchus
Luminal CSA of right principal bronchus—luminal CSA of left principal bronchus
Tracheal height at thoracic inlet–tracheal width at thoracic inlet
Long axis of the–short axis of the heart
Volume of the thoracic cavity–volume of the abdominal cavity

sec.p: The difference in size between right and left primary bronchus length and diameter were not statistically significant, and the right and left primary bronchus were equal in length and diameter (Tables 3 and 4) (p > 0.05). The difference in size between right and left primary bronchus luminal CSA was statistically significant, and the right primary bronchus had larger luminal CSA (Tables 3 and 4) (p = 0.007).

According to the measurements, 20.05% of the ventral cavity volume (thoracic cavity+ abdominal cavity) was thoracic cavity and 79.95% was abdominal cavity. According to the measurements, 62.28% of the thoracic cavity volume was related to the lungs and 14.04% to the heart.

sec.title: DISCUSSIONS
sec.p: In this study, the position of thoracic structures in guinea pigs was investigated using the CT scan method and it was confirmed and compared with the anatomical investigation. Knowledge of anatomy is essential to examine the condition of healthy thorax compositions and is beneficial in the early diagnosis of the disease. Restrictions on the evaluation of internal organs in living organisms have led to diagnostic imaging, including CT scans, playing an essential role in these conditions. Besides, methods such as CT scan can be used to examine the anatomical construction of organs when they are alive and active.

In the ringed seal, it is reported that the trachea is dorsoventrally flattened and compressed; this feature is probably due to their aquatic nature. This condition was not observed in guinea pigs. It should be mentioned that the tracheal height and width at the thoracic inlet were very close to each other and there was no statistically significant difference, and the cross‐section of the trachea in this area had a circular appearance (Smodlaka et al., 2009).

It has been noted that in African giant pouched rat (Cricetomys gambianus), the trachea dilates before bifurcation. While this condition was not observed in hamster, nor was it observed in guinea pigs in our study, it is necessary to study this condition from a morphometric point of view (Ibe et al., 2011).

In an anatomical study of white‐breasted hedgehogs (Erinaceus concolor), the left lung had only one lobe, and stretched from the 1st to the 14th ribs. The right lung was located in the white‐breasted hedgehog and included the cranial, middle, caudal and accessory lobes. But in the guinea pig, the left lung was three‐part and started near the 3rd rib and ended before the 11th rib. In guinea pigs, the right lung was similar to the southern white‐breasted hedgehog. The right lung began at the caudal edge of the 3rd rib and ended near the 12th rib. In general, the left lung started before the right lung and ended earlier. The exact location of each of the right lung lobes could not be assessed separately by CT scan (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). Popesko et al. (1992) reported that the guinea pig's lung lobulation reported the same, but the range of the left lung was from the front of the 2nd rib to the proximity of the 10th rib and the range of the right lung from the front to the 2nd rib after the 11th rib. The interpretation of this difference can be attributed to the time of the study, which is done after death.

In the southern white‐breasted hedgehog, the heart was stretched between the second and seventh ribs and placed obliquely (craniodorsal‐caudoventral) on the sternum (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). In the guinea pig, the cranial border of the heart began in the vicinity of rib four and continued until rib 8, and its placement was similar to that of the southern white‐breasted hedgehog, except the heart was almost in the middle line, and its apex was not inclined to the left.

In the white‐breasted hedgehog, the trachea was entered from the midline of the body into the thoracic cavity and mediastinum and was bifurcated at the back of the heart (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). Also, in the study on New Zealand White rabbit rabbit by Müllhaupt et al., the site of bifurcation of the trachea was reported in the space between the fourth and fifth ribs. In this case, the trachea started from the midline, around the first rib, and split into two at the 5th rib region. In the white‐breasted hedgehog, the trachea deviated to the right and split in two at the same location. The right bronchus was shorter. The extension of the bronchi into the lungs was caudodorsal. The same was observed in guinea pigs, except that the tracheal bifurcation site was almost on the midline and slightly inclined to the right. The right bronchus was shorter than the left. The right bronchus enters the lung near the 6th rib on its caudal edge, and the left bronchus enters the lung at the proximity of the 7th rib. The extension of the right and left bronchi after separation from the bifurcation site of the trachea was caudodorsal (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011; Müllhaupt et al. 2017). In the case of the hamster, it has been mentioned that the trachea bifurcates at the level of the sixth rib. But bifurcation in African giant pouched rat (Cricetomys gambianus) is done at the level of the third rib (Ibe et al., 2011).

White‐breasted hedgehogs had 15 ribs, including 10 sternal ribs, 3 asternal ribs and 2 floating ribs (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). The guinea pig had 14 pairs of ribs, including 6 sternal ribs and 8 asternal ribs. Five of 8 false ribs were floating. The first ribs were slightly wider than the other ribs. Popesko et al. (1992) reported the number of ribs as 13 pairs including 5 sternal pairs and 8 asternal pairs (5 floating pairs).

Thoracic cavity in carnivore, equide, ruminants (Berry et al., 2007; Nickel et al., 1981) and most rodents is flattened on the sides and is frustoconical. Thoracic cavity in mole rats (Spalax leucodon) is flattened on the top (Ilgun et al., 2014). Thoracic cavity in white‐breasted hedgehogs is also flattened on the sides (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). In the present study, in Indian guinea pigs, the thoracic cavity was also compressed laterally.

As mentioned in this study, in the guinea pig, the tracheal bifurcation is in the vicinity of the 5th intercostal space; a similar situation has been observed in rats. It was seen that bifurcation corresponds to the 4th intercostal space in the mole rat; it was reported at the 3rd intercostal space in African pouched rats and in opossums and 6th intercostal space in hamsters (Ilgun et al., 2014).

As mentioned in this study, the angle between the bronchi at the tracheal bifurcation is 47.98° ± 0.12° in the guinea pig. Of course, in humans, it is mentioned that this angle can vary between 40° and 80°. It is also mentioned in the rat that this angle is 64.78° ± 25.16° (Ilgun et al., 2014). Besides, it has been mentioned that abnormal angles can reduce the volume of the lungs (Canals et al., 2004). It should be noted that this difference, observed in this angle between guinea pig and rat, can be due to the difference in their metabolism rate.

Accessory lobe of the right lung is the smallest lobe in opossums, rats, polar foxes and dogs. In blind mice, the smallest lobe is the middle lobe (Ilgun et al., 2014). Studies have shown that the right lung is larger than the left lung, and the caudal lobe of the right lung is the largest lung lobe in many animals (Ilgun et al., 2014). Kazemi‐Darabadi et al. pointed out that the volume of the right lung on the southern white thoracic cavity hedgehogs is more than the left. The right lung was also longer, wider and taller than the left lung. In general, the lungs were stretched from the second to the last rib, but the right lung was slightly more extensive in the cranial and caudal region (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). In our study on the guinea pigs, it was found that, unlike the animals mentioned, the right and left lungs were approximately the same width, height and volume but in terms of location, the right lung was more caudal than the left lung. It seems that one of the reasons for the reluctance of the heart to the left and its placement on the midline in the guinea pig is that the volume of the two lungs is equal. In the study of Müllhaupt et al. (2017) on New Zealand White rabbits, the volume of the left lung was significantly higher than the volume of the left lung. In addition, it should be mentioned that in guinea pigs, the length of the right lung was longer than the left. In our study on the guinea pigs, one of the notable cases for the difference in the size of the density of the right and left caudal lung lobe can be due to the difference in the amount of air in them, according to the cases mentioned in the references. The more negative density of a part of the lung can be due to more air; it may be in that section (Kazerooni & Gross, 2004).

Although in most animals, the smallest lobe of the lung is the accessory lobe; in the case of possum, rat and arctic fox, the middle lobe is the smallest lobe of the lung (Ilgun et al., 2014). Based on our observations in the anatomical study of lung lobes in guinea pig, the smallest lobe of the lung is the accessory lobe. It must be mentioned that in this study, the volumes of different parts of the right and left lobes were not measured separately. Regarding the class Mammalia, it is mentioned that the presence of an accessory lobe in the right lung is a plesiomorphic feature (Ilgun et al., 2014). In the case of the African giant pouched rat, it has been mentioned that due to the topography and the way the lungs are positioned in relation to the heart, it is possible to take intracardiac blood samples from the right third intercoastal space; according to our observations in this study, it seems that this approach can also be used in guinea pigs (Ilgun et al., 2014).

Reports indicate that the groove between the lobes is deep in rodents and their right lung lobes are completely separate (Cope et al., 2001; Teke, 1991). Therefore, the right lung consists of four lobes (cranial, middle, caudal and appendix). This classification is expressed in hedgehogs, opossum (Cope et al., 2001), rabbit (Tillery & Lehnertb, 1986), guinea pig, rat, dog, cat and sheep (Nickel, 1981). The left lung has two lobes in rabbits (Teke, 1991), one lobe in rats and pouched rats, and Hazel dormice (Valerius, 1996), and three lobes in ruminants and carnivores (Nickel et al., 1981). In guinea pigs, the right lung consists of four lobes and the left lung has three lobes. In large mammals, the heart is located in the lower ventral part of the thorax and in the middle mediastinum (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). In most animals, the heart is stretched by a sharp head. Dogs' hearts are oval in shape, and the apex of the heart is slow and wide. Heart in ruminants is conical with sharp apex. The heart in sheep and pigs has a wide, blunt tip that inclines toward the middle. Rabbits have a pointed, elongated, conical heart (Nickel et al., 1981). In guinea pigs, the heart is almost round and the apex is not very pointed. However, the heart of these rodents is commensurate with the size of the thoracic cavity, and it takes up a lot of space in the thoracic cavity and leaves little space for the lungs on each side. The orientation of the heart in hedgehogs is similar to that of other animals, and the heart has a round apex in them (Kazemi‐Darabadi et al., 2011). But in the guinea pig, the apex of the heart was almost on the midline. It has been mentioned that the normal position of the apex of the heart in dogs and cats is always on the left in the ventrodorsal view/plane. In a study on rabbits, it is pointed out that in many cases, the apex tends to be more towards the midline than towards the left side (Müllhaupt et al., 2017; Rudorf et al., 2008).

The bronchial branching in this study for guinea pigs is similar to that mentioned in the studies of Schreider and Hutchens. In addition, in this study, the topography of the divisions performed was also considered and described (Schreider & Hutchens, 1980)

Regarding the rabbit, it is mentioned that the cranial range of the right lung is T3 or T4 and its caudal range is T11 or T12. In our study on guinea pigs, the cranial region of the right lung was slightly caudal to T3 and the caudal region was T11 (Müllhaupt et al., 2017; Rudorf et al., 2008). Regarding the rabbit's left lung, it is mentioned that the cranial range is T3 or T4 and its caudal range is T10 and T11. In the study on guinea pigs, the cranial area of the left lung is T3 and its caudal area is around T11 (Müllhaupt et al., 2017; Rudorf et al., 2008).

Relative cardiac height in rabbit is 74% and it is mentioned that this parameter in rabbit is a bigger number than dog and cat. In this study, this parameter was 73.18% in guinea pigs (Johnson et al., 2008; Müllhaupt et al., 2017). Relative cardiac width in rabbit is 88% and it is mentioned that this parameter in rabbit is a bigger number than dog and cat. In this study, this parameter was 86.01% in guinea pigs (Johnson et al., 2008; Müllhaupt et al., 2017).
In the case of rabbits, it is mentioned that the angle of trachea to spine is small; this angle is 5° in rabbits and 7.15° in guinea pigs (Müllhaupt et al., 2017).

Regarding values for tracheal height in relation to the thoracic height at the thoracic inlet in rabbits, it is mentioned that the size of this parameter is similar to nonbrachycephalic dogs. The average of this parameter was 25% for rabbits and 22.09% for guinea pigs (Müllhaupt et al., 2017).

Based on various references and studies in many animals, it has been pointed out that the right lung is larger than the left lung, and the caudal lobe is the largest lobe in the right lung (Ilgun et al., 2014). According to studies on guinea pigs, the right and left lungs are about the same volume, and the heart is positioned between them on the midline without a tendency to the left. This lack of inclination to one side can be due to the equal volume of the lungs.

Luminal CSA of right principal bronchus was larger than that of left principal bronchus in guinea pig as well as rabbit. Of course, it should be noted that in guinea pigs, the right and left lung volumes are equal, unlike rabbits (Müllhaupt et al., 2017). It should be noted that the length of the right and left bronchus was not different in guinea pigs.

Regarding the density of right and left caudal lung in rabbits, it is mentioned that the size of this parameter is higher than that of dogs (Morandi et al., 2003; Müllhaupt et al., 2017). It should be mentioned that this parameter is larger in guinea pigs than in dogs. The size of this parameter is different in the right and left lungs of rabbits and guinea pigs (Müllhaupt et al., 2017). One of the reasons for this condition in rabbits and guinea pigs compared to dogs is compression of the lung, secondary to a large abdomen (Müllhaupt et al., 2017).

Regarding relative cardiac height and in particular cardiac width in rabbits, it is mentioned that the size of these two parameters is greater than that of dogs and cats. In the case of guinea pigs, both numerical parameters are smaller than rabbits, but close to it (Müllhaupt et al., 2017).

In this study, some similarities and differences between the lungs and thoracic cavity in guinea pigs and other mammals were investigated, and CT scan was used as one of the best diagnostic imaging techniques for anatomical and topographical studies of the respiratory system in living animals. It is suggested to carry out additional studies using this method in other animals and in future studies to measure the volume of each part of the right and left lung lobes. It is necessary to reemphasise that the use of diagnostic imaging techniques for anatomical studies causes fewer live animals to be euthanised in these studies. In addition to this, the accuracy of the study increases because the study is performed while the structures under investigation are alive and active.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Omid Zehtabvar: conceptualisation; data curation; formal analysis; funding acquisition; investigation; methodology; project administration; resources; software; supervision; validation; visualisation; writing – original draft; writing – review & editing. Majid Masoudifard: conceptualisation; data curation; formal analysis; funding acquisition; investigation; methodology; project administration; resources; software; supervision; validation; visualisation; writing – original draft; writing – review & editing. Amir Rostami: data curation; methodology; resources. Fakhra Borgheie: formal analysis.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: This study was a DVM thesis and all experimental procedures were approved by the Faculty of Veterinary medicine, University of Tehran Local Ethics Committee (87082).

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1131.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: The authors wish to express their appreciation to everyone that assists us in this study. Authors would like to acknowledge to fully financial support of Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran for this research.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: Data available on request from the authors.
